{"query":"H5N1 confirmed human infections deaths January-August 2025 global total by country","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1618,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/global-summary-06262026.html","title":"Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from August 2025 to June 2026 | Bird Flu | CDC","content":"by S Flu — From August 4, 2025 to June 10, 2026, 12 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) were reported by three countries ...Read more
From August 4, 2025 to June 10, 2026, 12 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) were reported by three countries outside of the United States.
There are many different subtypes of avian influenza (bird flu) viruses; A(H5N1) viruses have accounted for most human cases reported globally since 1997.
Since August 2025, there have been an additional 12 cases of H5N1 bird flu reported internationally.
As of June 10, 2026, 1,022 cases have now been reported worldwide since 1997.
Most A(H5N1) virus infections in other countries have been associated with a different clade (i.e., genetic grouping) of A(H5N1) viruses than cases reported in the United States, and generally have a high mortality rate.
India has reported one human case of H5N1 bird flu since CDC's August 2025 summary.
Backyard poultry in the village with two isolated poultry deaths suggests a possible environmental exposure.
While rare, these H5N1 bird flu cases in humans underscore the need for strong systems to monitor and prepare for influenza, including robust surveillance and testing, to understand which influenza viruses are circulating and their effect on human health."},{"url":"https://www.fao.org/animal-health/situation-updates/global-aiv-with-zoonotic-potential/en","title":"Global AIV with Zoonotic Potential","content":"H5Nx, H7Nx high pathogenicity avian influenza (HPAI) viruses and H3N8, H5Nx, H6N1, H7Nx, H9N2, H10Nx and H11 low pathogenicity avian influenza (LPAI).
HPAI outbreaks in animals officially reported since last update (23 April 2026): in total, 763 outbreaks / events have been reported in 31 countries/territories caused by H5Nx (95), H5N1 (659), H5N5 (3), H5N9 (3) and H7N3 (3) (see Table 1 for details).

Emergence of D1.1 reassortant H5N1 avian influenza viruses in North America. bioRxiv, 2026 Apr 20:2025.12.19.695329.
Since early 2025, HPAI events have been confirmed by the national veterinary laboratories in Nigeria (n=45), Ghana (n=1), Liberia (n=1), Niger (n=3), and Togo (n=4).
In early 2025, Togo confirmed the presence of HPAI H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b, a new genotype which had not been previously reported in the country, a highest genetic similarity was found with H5N1 sequences from Ghana and Israel.
FAO Viet Nam is conducting cross-border poultry value chain analysis in four border provinces including Tuyen Quang, Ha Tinh, Dong Thap and An Giang.
To date, more than 140 influenza A(H9N2) human cases diagnosed worldwide, many of them were reported from China since December 2015."},{"url":"https://www.who.int/teams/global-influenza-programme/avian-influenza/avian-a-h5n1-virus","title":"Global Influenza Programme","content":"Infections in humans can cause severe disease with a high mortality rate.
This virus does not appear to transmit easily from person to person, and sustained human-to-human transmission has not been reported.

tavily.raw: {"query":"H5N1 confirmed human infections deaths January-August 2025 global total by country","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/h5n1-summary-08042025.html","title":"Global Summary of Recent Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu","content":"A **.gov** website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. # Global Summary of Recent Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu. *   Between January 1 and August 4, 2025, 26 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5 bird flu) have been detected globally, of which 23 were identified in 7 countries outside of the United States, including 11 infections that resulted in death. *   Due to widespread global outbreaks of H5N1 bird flu in wild birds and poultry, sporadic human infections with H5N1 virus are not surprising in persons with direct or close contact with sick or dead poultry or other infected animals, but they do underscore the importance of continued surveillance domestically and globally. CDC continues to work with global partners to identify and respond to human infections with avian influenza A viruses, like H5N1 viruses, which caused outbreaks in poultry, dairy cows and other animals and 70 human cases in the United States during 2024 and early 2025.","score":0.93735445,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/global-summary-06262026.html","title":"Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from ...","content":"A **.gov** website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. # Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from August 2025 to June 2026. *   From August 4, 2025 to June 10, 2026, 12 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) were reported by three countries outside of the United States. *   The 12 cases were reported by Bangladesh, Cambodia, and India. This report focuses on human cases of H5N1 bird flu reported internationally since August 4, 2025, when CDC last published a summary of human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) reported internationally. These A(H5N1) virus infections occurred in three countries: Bangladesh, Cambodia, and India. Most A(H5N1) virus infections in other countries have been associated with a different clade (i.e., genetic grouping) of A(H5N1) viruses than cases reported in the United States, and generally have a high mortality rate. Three human bird flu cases with A(H5N1) virus infection (clade 2.3.2.1a) were reported to have occurred in Bangladesh since August 2025.","score":0.8947989,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2025-DON575","title":"Avian Influenza A(H5N1) - Cambodia","content":"# Avian Influenza A(H5N1) - Cambodia. Between 1 January and 1 July 2025, the World Health Organization (WHO) was notified by Cambodia’s International Health Regulations (IHR) National Focal Point (NFP) of 11 laboratory-confirmed cases of human infection with avian influenza A(H5N1) virus. Since then, 83 cases of human infection with influenza A(H5N1), including 49 deaths (case fatality ratio [CFR] of 59%), have been reported in the country. Since the re-emergence of human A(H5N1) infections in Cambodia in 2023, a total of 27 cases have been reported (six in 2023, 10 in 2024, and 11 to date in 2025), of which 12 were fatal (CFR 44%). Avian influenza A(H5N1) is circulating in wild birds, poultry and some mammals around the world, and occasional human infections following exposure to infected animals or contaminated environments are expected to occur. According to the IHR, a human infection caused by a novel influenza A virus subtype is an event that has the potential for high public health impact and must be notified to the WHO.","score":0.8747745,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.fao.org/animal-health/situation-updates/global-aiv-with-zoonotic-potential/en","title":"Global AIV with Zoonotic Potential","content":"## Animal health. ### **Global Avian Influenza Viruses with Zoonotic Potential situation update**. This update covers avian influenza viruses (AIV) with zoonotic potential occurring worldwide, i.e. H5Nx, H7Nx high pathogenicity avian influenza (HPAI) viruses and H3N8, H5Nx, H6N1, H7Nx, H9N2, H10Nx and H11 low pathogenicity avian influenza (LPAI). Specific information is available for Avian Influenza A(H7N9) virus-virus/en) viruses and Sub-Saharan Africa HPAI  in related FAO Avian Influenza situation updates. **HPAI outbreaks in animals** officially reported since last update (23 April 2026): in total, 763 outbreaks / events have been reported in 31 countries/territories caused by H5Nx (95), H5N1 (659), H5N5 (3), H5N9 (3) and H7N3 (3) (see Table 1 for details). **Number of human cases** officially reported since the last update: 5 new events were reported. **Map 2.** *Global distribution of AIV with zoonotic potential\\* observed in the period 1 October 2024 to 30 September 2025 (i.e. previous wave)*. **Table 1.**High pathogenicity avian influenza viruses with zoonotic potential reported **since the last update**.","score":0.6334335,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/php/surveillance/chart-epi-curve-ah5n1.html","title":"Global Human Cases with Avian Influenza A(H5N1), 1997- ...","content":"overall 18 people were confirmed with HPAI A(H5N1) virus infection, including 6 deaths. 25 countries, including the United States, have","score":0.49641174,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.03,"request_id":"112a85c8-4bfa-4aaa-8914-91fea948def6"}
